message:
feat(config): support FRANKENTERM_CONFIG_FILE env var for explicit config path

Add support for explicitly specifying the TOML configuration file path
via the FRANKENTERM_CONFIG_FILE environment variable. When set to a path
ending in .toml, it takes priority over the standard search path
discovery logic.

toml_config.rs:
- Add explicit_toml_config_path_from_env() to read FRANKENTERM_CONFIG_FILE
- Add is_toml_path() helper for extension validation
- Wire env-based path into try_load_toml_config() as first-priority check
- Return clear error messages when the specified file doesn't exist

wasm_config.rs:
- Mirror the same FRANKENTERM_CONFIG_FILE support for WASM builds
- Add equivalent is_toml_path() and explicit path resolution

.beads/issues.jsonl:
- Update issue tracker metadata

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -142,7 +142,7 @@
 {"id":"ft-1m2","title":"Explanation templates: reusable reason patterns for wa why and errors","description":"# Explanation Templates: Reusable reason patterns for wa why

## Purpose
Provide consistent, helpful explanations for common scenarios via template system.

## Implementation

### Template Structure
```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub struct ExplanationTemplate {
    pub id: &'static str,
    pub scenario: &'static str,
    pub brief: &'static str,
    pub detailed: &'static str,
    pub suggestions: Vec<&'static str>,
    pub see_also: Vec<&'static str>,
}
```

### Built-in Templates

#### Policy Denials
```rust
pub static DENY_ALT_SCREEN: ExplanationTemplate = ExplanationTemplate {
    id: "deny.alt_screen",
    scenario: "Send denied because alt-screen is active",
    brief: "Pane is in full-screen mode (vim, less, etc.)",
    detailed: r#"
The pane is currently displaying an alternate screen buffer, which typically
means a full-screen application like vim, less, htop, or similar is running.

Sending text while alt-screen is active could:
- Corrupt the application state
- Cause unintended keystrokes
- Interfere with user interaction

The safety policy blocks sends to alt-screen panes by default.
"#,
    suggestions: vec![
        "Exit the full-screen application first",
        "Use --force if you're certain this is safe",
        "Configure policy to allow specific alt-screen apps",
    ],
    see_also: vec!["wa policy", "wa status --pane <id>"],
};

pub static DENY_COMMAND_RUNNING: ExplanationTemplate = ExplanationTemplate {
    id: "deny.command_running",
    scenario: "Send denied because a command is running",
    brief: "Another command is currently executing in the pane",
    detailed: r#"
The pane has an active command running (detected via OSC 133 markers or
heuristics). Sending text while a command runs could:

- Interrupt the running command
- Queue input for later (confusing)
- Cause the shell to misinterpret input

wa waits for command completion before sending unless overridden.
"#,
    suggestions: vec![
        "Wait for the current command to finish",
        "Use Ctrl-C to cancel the running command first",
        "Use --wait-for to send after a specific pattern",
    ],
    see_also: vec!["wa status", "wa send --wait-for"],
};

pub static DENY_RECENT_GAP: ExplanationTemplate = ExplanationTemplate {
    id: "deny.recent_gap",
    scenario: "Send denied due to recent output gap",
    brief: "Pane had no output recently, possibly waiting for input",
    detailed: r#"
wa detected a gap in pane output that suggests the pane might be:
- Waiting for user input at a prompt
- Displaying a confirmation dialog
- In an unknown state

The policy requires a prompt marker (OSC 133) or manual confirmation.
"#,
    suggestions: vec![
        "Check the pane manually to see its state",
        "Use --force if you've verified the pane is ready",
        "Enable OSC 133 support in your shell for better detection",
    ],
    see_also: vec!["wa capabilities --pane <id>"],
};
```

#### Workflow Explanations
```rust
pub static WORKFLOW_USAGE_LIMIT: ExplanationTemplate = ExplanationTemplate {
    id: "workflow.usage_limit",
    scenario: "Why handle_usage_limits workflow was triggered",
    brief: "Codex hit its daily token usage limit",
    detailed: r#"
The Codex agent reported it has reached its usage limit. This typically
happens when:

- Daily token quota exceeded
- Account-level rate limiting triggered

The handle_usage_limits workflow will:
1. Gracefully exit the current Codex session
2. Parse the session summary for resume ID
3. Select an alternate OpenAI account
4. Complete device auth flow
5. Resume the session with new credentials
"#,
    suggestions: vec![
        "Let the workflow complete automatically",
        "Check account status with: caut status",
        "Configure account pool in wa.toml",
    ],
    see_also: vec!["wa workflow status", "caut"],
};
```

### Template Registry
```rust
lazy_static! {
    pub static ref EXPLANATION_TEMPLATES: HashMap<&'static str, &'static ExplanationTemplate> = {
        let mut m = HashMap::new();
        m.insert("deny.alt_screen", &DENY_ALT_SCREEN);
        m.insert("deny.command_running", &DENY_COMMAND_RUNNING);
        m.insert("deny.recent_gap", &DENY_RECENT_GAP);
        m.insert("workflow.usage_limit", &WORKFLOW_USAGE_LIMIT);
        // ... more templates
        m
    };
}

pub fn get_explanation(id: &str) -> Option<&'static ExplanationTemplate> {
    EXPLANATION_TEMPLATES.get(id).copied()
}
```

### Template Interpolation
```rust
pub fn render_explanation(
    template: &ExplanationTemplate,
    context: &HashMap<String, String>,
) -> String {
    let mut output = template.detailed.to_string();
    for (key, value) in context {
        output = output.replace(&format!("{{{}}}", key), value);
    }
    output
}
```

## Testing
- All templates have valid structure
- Interpolation works correctly
- No broken cross-references

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Templates for all common denial reasons
- [ ] Templates for all workflow triggers
- [ ] wa why uses templates consistently
- [ ] Templates documented in help

-{"id":"ft-1m7nk","title":"Refactor runtime.rs async observation loops to asupersync","description":"# Refactor runtime.rs async observation loops to asupersync

## Background
runtime.rs (~500+ LOC) contains main observation loops. Uses 38+ mpsc channel instances, select!, RwLock, background spawns.

## Migration
1. Thread Cx through the main event loop
2. Replace all mpsc channels with asupersync channels (two-phase send)
3. Replace select!-based event loop with asupersync::combinator::select
4. Replace RwLock with asupersync::sync::RwLock
5. Replace background spawns with scope.spawn inside regions
6. Replace adaptive sleep with cx.sleep

## Design: Nested regions for structured lifecycle
- Outer region: application lifetime scope
- Inner regions: per-observation-cycle (allows clean cancellation per cycle)

## Unit tests (LabRuntime)
1. **Event loop startup/shutdown**: verify clean lifecycle
2. **Channel dispatch**: send command, verify observation loop processes it
3. **Multi-channel select**: concurrent events on multiple channels, verify all handled
4. **Adaptive polling**: verify sleep intervals adjust correctly (virtual time)
5. **Shutdown propagation**: cancel outer scope, verify all inner scopes drain
6. **RwLock contention**: concurrent readers + writer, verify no deadlocks
7. **Backpressure**: overflow channel, verify bounded behavior

Each test logs: event loop iterations, channel receives, select outcomes, sleep durations.

## Acceptance criteria
- Main observation loop works with asupersync
- All 38+ channel instances migrated (two-phase send)
- Event dispatch latency not regressed
- Clean shutdown via scope draining
- 7+ LabRuntime tests

## LabRuntime DPOR
- **Observation loop testing with DPOR**: Use LabRuntime schedule exploration (DPOR) to test observation loop concurrency:
  - Events arriving on multiple channels simultaneously — verify all events processed, none dropped
  - Interleaved select resolution and scope.spawn — verify no races between event handling and task creation
  - Shutdown signal arriving during mid-cycle processing — verify clean drain of in-flight work
  - RwLock writer starvation scenarios — verify writers eventually acquire under sustained reader load
  - Nested region teardown ordering — verify inner observation cycles complete before outer scope exits

## Benchmark requirements
- **Criterion benchmarks for loop overhead**: Add `benches/observation_loop.rs` measuring:
  - Single event dispatch latency: event sent on channel → processed by observation loop
  - Multi-channel select resolution latency: time to select across N=2,4,8,16 channels
  - Adaptive sleep overhead: cx.sleep() call cost during idle periods
  - Event throughput: events/sec sustained through the observation loop
  - Comparison with tokio select!-based loop for equivalent event rates","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CoralMeadow","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:50:30.290899Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-20T12:53:17.527448Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-1m7nk","depends_on_id":"ft-1bznu","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-15T21:29:20Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-1m7nk","depends_on_id":"ft-2abzy","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-15T21:29:20Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":50,"issue_id":"ft-1m7nk","author":"CoralMeadow","text":"Starting implementation slice for wa-1m7nk under wa-1bznu. Scope: migrate runtime observation loop internals off direct tokio sync primitives where non-invasive; first pass targets runtime.rs lock primitive migration + compile verification.","created_at":"2026-02-14T04:16:21Z"},{"id":51,"issue_id":"ft-1m7nk","author":"CoralMeadow","text":"Completed first runtime migration slice: switched runtime observation module to  import and replaced two simple sleep/recv  loops with  waits in  and . Validation:  and  (pass; existing non-fatal warnings only).","created_at":"2026-02-14T04:20:21Z"},{"id":52,"issue_id":"ft-1m7nk","author":"CoralMeadow","text":"Correction to previous note (shell stripped inline code markers): completed first runtime migration slice in crates/frankenterm-core/src/runtime.rs. Changes: replaced direct tokio::sync::RwLock import with runtime_compat::RwLock; replaced two sleep/recv tokio::select loops with runtime_compat::timeout waits in spawn_snapshot_trigger_task and spawn_capture_relay_task. Validation: rustfmt --edition 2024 --check crates/frankenterm-core/src/runtime.rs; CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target-coral-wa1m7nk cargo check -p frankenterm-core --all-targets (pass, warnings only).","created_at":"2026-02-14T04:20:39Z"},{"id":53,"issue_id":"ft-1m7nk","author":"CoralMeadow","text":"Added second runtime migration slice in crates/frankenterm-core/src/runtime.rs: refactored native event coalescer loop from tokio::select to timeout-driven control flow with explicit periodic flush checkpoints, preserving shutdown and flush semantics under sustained event throughput. Validation rerun: rustfmt --edition 2024 --check crates/frankenterm-core/src/runtime.rs; CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target-coral-wa1m7nk cargo check -p frankenterm-core --all-targets (pass, warnings only).","created_at":"2026-02-14T04:22:50Z"},{"id":538,"issue_id":"ft-1m7nk","author":"TealFalcon","text":"TealFalcon starting a narrow non-overlapping support slice: remove remaining tokio::select usage in runtime capture loop (runtime.rs) by switching to runtime_compat timeout+tick scheduling, preserving cadence/behavior while advancing wa-1m7nk migration.","created_at":"2026-02-20T12:45:52Z"},{"id":542,"issue_id":"ft-1m7nk","author":"TealFalcon","text":"Completed focused runtime.rs slice: removed remaining tokio::select! from capture loop (spawn_capture_task) and replaced with runtime-compat scheduling using sleep/timeout + explicit next_sync_tick/next_spawn_tick deadlines. Behavior preserved: immediate first sync tick, periodic sync cadence, 10ms spawn-ready cadence, poll result handling, graceful shutdown. Validation: rch exec -- rustfmt --edition 2024 --check crates/frankenterm-core/src/runtime.rs (pass); rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target-tealfalcon-runtime cargo check -p frankenterm-core --lib (pass). Note: rch remote workers were selected but SSH connect failed and fell back to local execution.","created_at":"2026-02-20T12:53:17Z"}]}

 {"id":"ft-1ms1","title":"Update all dependencies to latest stable versions","description":"## Dependency Updates Required

Closed Dependabot PRs:
- 12 rust dependency updates (grouped rust-deps)
- 5 GH Actions updates (actions group)

## Steps
1. Run cargo update
2. Run: cargo check --all-targets && cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings
3. Run: cargo test
4. Update GH Actions versions in .github/workflows/*.yml
5. Run: actionlint .github/workflows/*.yml

## Testing
- cargo test must pass
- cargo clippy must pass with -D warnings
- CI must pass on push

## Post-Update Audit

After all dependency updates are applied, run `cargo audit` to check for known security vulnerabilities in the updated dependency tree. This is essential because:
- A newer version of a transitive dependency may have introduced a vulnerability since the last audit.
- Dependabot groups updates, so multiple crate versions change at once -- each must be checked.
- If `cargo audit` reports any findings, evaluate severity and either pin the affected crate to a safe version or document the accepted risk.

Steps:
1. `cargo install cargo-audit` (if not already installed)
2. `cargo audit`
3. Address any RUSTSEC advisories before merging the update PR
4. Consider adding `cargo audit` to CI if not already present

## Cross-References
- **wa-brc7d** (asupersync migration): The asupersync crate (used for async-to-sync bridging) has specific version compatibility requirements. When updating dependencies, verify that the updated versions of tokio, futures, and async-trait remain compatible with asupersync's expected async runtime behavior. Run asupersync-specific integration tests after the update to confirm no regressions in the sync bridge layer.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T00:22:32.410353769Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T13:06:56.659545Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T13:06:56.659532Z","close_reason":"Implemented in f8964319. cargo update bumped 29 packages (cc, libc, asupersync, fastapi, fastmcp, ftui git deps). All workspace tests pass.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-1nbo","title":"Metrics + diagnostics for capture throttling","description":"## What
Expose metrics and CLI diagnostics showing capture budgets and throttling decisions.

## Why
Without visibility, operators can't tune priorities safely.

## How
- Add counters for throttled captures, skipped panes
- Extend `wa status/health` to show budget state

## Success Criteria
- Metrics are exposed (Prometheus or JSON)
- Status output includes reasoned throttling info","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:05:09.174601153Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-11T05:47:48.172215Z","closed_at":"2026-02-07T00:19:09.817138668Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-1nbo","depends_on_id":"ft-9ke1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-15T21:29:20Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-1nbo","depends_on_id":"ft-p9em","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-15T21:29:20Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"ft-3k2g1","title":"Make luahelper crate fully optional behind lua feature","description":"# Make luahelper Crate Fully Optional

## Context (revised from "Remove luahelper dependency")
Under the dual-runtime strategy, luahelper stays in the tree but becomes optional. When the `lua` feature is disabled, no crate should depend on luahelper.

## Crates that currently depend on luahelper
1. config — for Lua config parsing → gate behind `lua` feature
2. mux — for Lua domain management → gate behind `lua` feature
3. procinfo — optional, already has lua feature → verify it's gated
4. termwiz-funcs — for Lua termwiz bindings → gate behind `lua` feature

## What to do
1. Verify each crate's luahelper dependency is behind `lua` feature flag
2. Ensure luahelper itself only compiles when at least one dependent enables `lua`
3. Add workspace-level feature: `workspace-lua = ["config/lua", "mux/lua", ...]`
4. Test: with no-lua, luahelper should not appear in `cargo tree` output

## Success criteria
- `cargo check --no-default-features` compiles without luahelper in dep tree
- `cargo check` (default, with lua) still includes luahelper and works
- `cargo tree -p config --no-default-features | grep luahelper` returns nothing
- All existing Lua tests pass when lua feature is enabled

## Why not remove entirely
luahelper contains useful Rust↔Lua bridging code. Users who want Lua scripting (backward compat with wezterm.lua) still need it. Removing it would break the Lua path.

## Test Framework Requirements
- **Proptest**: Property-based tests for feature flag combinations:
  - Generate random subsets of feature flags from {lua, wasm, no-lua, frankenterm, vendored} and verify that cargo check succeeds for all valid combinations
  - Verify that incompatible combinations (e.g., lua + no-lua simultaneously) are rejected at compile time or produce clear errors
  - For each valid feature combination, verify luahelper appears in `cargo tree` if and only if `lua` is enabled

## Cross-References
- **wa-ggw9w** (Config crate Lua optional): The config crate's `lua` feature flag must be coordinated with luahelper's optionality. When config disables `lua`, its luahelper dep must disappear.
- **wa-2ufom** (Mux crate Lua optional): Same coordination needed for the mux crate — its luahelper dep must be gated behind `lua`.
- Together, wa-3k2g1 + wa-ggw9w + wa-2ufom form the complete "Lua-optional" triad. All three must be implemented together for a clean no-Lua build.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"ClaudeOpus4.6-Session","created_at":"2026-02-10T06:52:48.067258Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-13T11:32:18.157823Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T11:32:18.157808Z","close_reason":"Completed: termwiz-funcs feature-gated behind lua. luahelper/mlua are optional deps. All Lua code gated with #[cfg(feature=lua)]. Verified with cargo check/tree.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-3k2g1","depends_on_id":"ft-2ufom","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-15T21:29:20Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-3k2g1","depends_on_id":"ft-ggw9w","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-15T21:29:20Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":227,"issue_id":"ft-3k2g1","author":"ClaudeOpus4.6-Session","text":"Completed: termwiz-funcs is now fully feature-gated behind lua. (3) Used path dep for config with default-features=false. Pure utility functions (pad_right, truncate, format_as_escapes, lines_to_escapes) remain available without lua.  {"id":"ft-3kal","title":"FTUI-04.2 Build adapter layer from QueryClient data to ftui view models","description":"## Background\nCurrent view structs are ratatui-oriented. We need explicit adapters that isolate rendering from backend schema evolution.\n\n## Deliverables\n- mapping modules for pane/event/triage/history/search/workflow views\n- normalization and redaction-safe formatting rules\n- unit tests on adapter transformations\n\n## Acceptance Criteria\n- adapters produce deterministic, render-ready view models\n- schema evolution does not require direct widget rewrites.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"PinkMountain","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:08:00.269912667Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor","updated_at":"2026-02-09T01:25:54.877233043Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T01:25:54.877099585Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-3kal","depends_on_id":"ft-1ssn","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-15T21:29:20Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-3kal","depends_on_id":"ft-5htt","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-15T21:29:20Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-3kal","depends_on_id":"ft-8q4e","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-15T21:29:20Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"ft-3kxe","title":"[EPIC] Fork Hardening — Fix WezTerm mux server root causes","description":"## Goal
Fix root causes in the forked WezTerm mux server that cause it to die under agent swarm load, moving beyond client-side workarounds to true reliability.

## Background & Motivation
From the user's X post: "I'm just so tired of WezTerm dying horribly under the extreme load of my agent swarm sessions and losing time and work. It just wasn't built for these assumptions."

The wa-3cyp performance epic focuses on CLIENT-side optimizations (polling, pooling, compression). This epic goes INTO THE WEZTERM CODE ITSELF to fix the root causes:
1. Memory leaks in the mux server (76GB RSS after 23 days observed)
2. No memory limits or OOM prevention
3. No incremental snapshot capability
4. Lock contention in hot paths

The user decided "I want to rename it to FrankenTerm and control the entire stack all the way for full control." This epic delivers on that promise by making the fork genuinely more reliable than upstream WezTerm for agent swarm workloads.

## Design Philosophy (Extreme Software Optimization)
Every change follows the ESO loop:
1. BASELINE — Measure current behavior (hyperfine, heaptrack)
2. PROFILE — Identify actual hotspots (not guesses)
3. PROVE — Golden outputs + isomorphism proof per change
4. IMPLEMENT — Only changes with Opportunity Score ≥ 2.0
5. VERIFY — sha256sum -c golden_checksums.txt
6. REPEAT — Re-profile (bottlenecks shift)

## Scope
This epic covers 5 targeted changes to the forked WezTerm mux server:
1. Memory leak root cause analysis and patches
2. cgroups memory budget and OOM prevention
3. Differential snapshots for continuous saving
4. Lock-free capture pipeline
5. Operational telemetry pipeline

## Relationship to Existing Beads
- Extends wa-3cyp (performance) — but focuses on WezTerm server code, not wa client
- Enables wa-rsaf (session persistence) — differential snapshots enable continuous saving
- Complements wa-2cha (watchdog) — OOM prevention adds another layer of protection

## Dependencies
- FrankenTerm rename (wa-vv3h) should ideally complete first (we're modifying forked code)
- No hard blockers — can start profiling immediately

## Acceptance Criteria
- Memory leak root causes identified and patched with isomorphism proofs
- cgroups memory budget prevents OOM with proactive save triggers
- Differential snapshots reduce save time to sub-100ms
- Lock-free capture eliminates contention in hot path
- Telemetry pipeline provides full observability
- All changes follow ESO loop (profile → prove → implement → verify)
- Comprehensive benchmark suite for regression detection","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","assignee":"CalmOwl","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:23:46.320024Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-14T18:12:02.478444Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":228,"issue_id":"ft-3kxe","author":"CalmOwl","text":"Claiming wa-3kxe at epic level to progress validation/benchmark scaffolding while dependent leak-fix work continues. Coordination posted on wa-3kxe.1 and wa-3kxe.6 to avoid overlap; I will focus on reusable hardening verification infrastructure.","created_at":"2026-02-14T17:54:37Z"},{"id":229,"issue_id":"ft-3kxe","author":"Jeff Emanuel","text":"Execution update: created and started wa-3kxe.7 to avoid blocker waits while wa-3kxe.1 remains in progress. Target output: new fork-hardening benchmark suite + enforceable budget prefixes, scoped to benchmark/test infra only.","created_at":"2026-02-14T17:59:45Z"},{"id":230,"issue_id":"ft-3kxe","author":"Jeff Emanuel","text":"Closed wa-3kxe.7: added fork-hardening benchmark harness + CI budget prefix mapping. This keeps wa-3kxe progressing while wa-3kxe.1/wa-3kxe.6 continue in parallel.","created_at":"2026-02-14T18:12:02Z"}]}
-{"id":"ft-3kxe.1","title":"Memory leak root cause analysis and patches (ESO methodology)","description":"## Goal
Systematically profile the WezTerm mux server under agent swarm load to identify and fix the root causes of memory leaks, using the ESO (Extreme Software Optimization) methodology with isomorphism proofs for every patch.

## Background & Motivation
The user observed 76GB RSS after 23 days of agent swarm operation. The mux server was never designed for this workload (50+ panes, continuous output, long-running). The leaks are likely in:
- Terminal state accumulation (cursor history, attribute stacks)
- Scrollback buffer growth (even with limits, internal fragmentation)
- Notification subscriber accumulation (Screen::set_dirty notifies ALL subscribers)
- Codec/parser state accumulation (incomplete escape sequences held in memory)
- Tab/pane metadata that grows but never shrinks

## Technical Approach (ESO Loop)

### Phase 1: Baseline
```bash
# Start mux server with 50 panes simulating agent output
hyperfine --warmup 1 --runs 3 'wezterm cli list-clients'

# Record baseline RSS over time
while true; do ps -o rss= -p $MUX_PID >> /tmp/rss_baseline.csv; sleep 60; done
```

### Phase 2: Profile
```bash
# Heap profiling (find allocations that never get freed)
heaptrack wezterm-mux-server --daemonize=false

# DHAT for allocation patterns
valgrind --tool=dhat wezterm-mux-server --daemonize=false

# Custom allocator with tracking
MALLOC_CONF="prof:true,prof_prefix:/tmp/jeprof" wezterm-mux-server
```

### Phase 3: Identify Top Allocators
From heaptrack output, identify:
- Top 5 allocation sites by total bytes (leaks)
- Top 5 allocation sites by count (fragmentation)
- Allocations that grow monotonically with time (unbounded growth)

### Phase 4: Patch with Isomorphism Proof
For each leak source:
```
## Change: Cap terminal attribute stack at 256 entries
- Ordering preserved:     yes — FIFO stack semantics unchanged
- Tie-breaking unchanged: yes — no ordering affected
- Floating-point:         N/A
- RNG seeds:              N/A
- Golden outputs:         sha256sum -c golden_checksums.txt ✓
```

### Phase 5: Verify
```bash
# Run same workload, compare RSS curve
# Must be within 10% of baseline at t=0h
# Must not grow faster than 1MB/hour with 50 active panes
```

## Expected Leak Sources (Hypotheses)
1. **Screen::set_dirty subscribers**: Each subscriber registration may not be cleaned up when panes are dropped
2. **Terminal::parser_state**: Incomplete escape sequence buffers may accumulate
3. **Scrollback internal fragmentation**: Vec<Line> with small allocations creating fragmentation
4. **TabState metadata**: Per-tab state that grows with pane operations
5. **Mux notification channels**: Unbounded channel buffers for pane events

## Implementation Location
- WezTerm source: wezterm-mux-server/ and mux/ crates
- Profiling scripts: scripts/profiling/
- Golden outputs: tests/golden/

## Configuration
N/A — this is analysis and patching work, not a runtime feature.

## Dependencies
- FrankenTerm fork (bd-20fw) should be set up first
- Profiling needs a Linux machine (heaptrack, DHAT) → trj or css

## Acceptance Criteria
- Top 5 memory leak sources identified with heaptrack evidence
- Each leak patched with isomorphism proof documenting behavior preservation
- RSS growth rate < 1MB/hour with 50 active panes (down from ~130MB/hour)
- Golden output tests pass before and after patches
- Profiling scripts committed for future regression detection
- Results documented: before/after RSS curves, allocation profiles

## macOS Compatibility (Leak Detection Tools)
On macOS, Valgrind and heaptrack are not available (or poorly supported). Use macOS-native alternatives:
- **`leaks` tool** (built-in): `leaks --atExit -- wezterm-mux-server` — reports all leaked allocations at process exit. Use `leaks --groupByType` for categorized output.
- **Instruments.app**: Use the "Leaks" and "Allocations" instruments for interactive profiling. Instruments provides allocation flame graphs, leak detection, and memory growth tracking equivalent to heaptrack.
- **`malloc_history`**: Track allocation history for specific addresses identified by `leaks`.
- **`vmmap`**: Analyze virtual memory regions to understand fragmentation and large allocations.
- **jemalloc prof on macOS**: jemalloc's profiling (`MALLOC_CONF="prof:true"`) works on macOS. Use `jeprof` to analyze heap profiles — this is the cross-platform option that works on both Linux and macOS.
- **Recommended workflow**: Use `leaks` + Instruments on macOS for initial triage, jemalloc prof for cross-platform profiling, and Valgrind/heaptrack on Linux (trj/css) for deep analysis. Compare results across platforms to catch platform-specific leaks.

## Test Framework Requirements
- **Criterion benchmarks**: Memory usage over time benchmarks:
  - Benchmark RSS growth rate: run mux server with 50 mock panes for 1000 capture cycles, measure RSS at start and end. Target <1KB/cycle growth.
  - Benchmark per-pane memory footprint: create panes with varying scrollback sizes (1K, 10K, 100K lines), measure memory per pane. Track this as a regression metric.
  - Benchmark allocation rate: measure allocations/sec during steady-state capture. Identify allocation-heavy code paths.

## Cross-References
- **wa-3axa** (Custom allocator): The custom allocator (jemalloc + per-pane arenas) is designed to mitigate the leaks this bead identifies. Root cause analysis here informs allocator arena design — if leaks are primarily in scrollback buffers, per-pane arenas should isolate scrollback allocations. If leaks are in shared state (subscribers, notification channels), the allocator needs a different strategy.","notes":"CreamTower takeover (stale assignee) for bounded-memory hardening + profiling evidence updates in non-overlapping mux/parser paths.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CreamTower","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:24:10.374379Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-20T17:34:08.911252Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-3kxe.1","depends_on_id":"ft-20fw","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-15T21:29:20Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-3kxe.1","depends_on_id":"ft-3kxe","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-15T21:29:20Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-3kxe.1","depends_on_id":"ft-ixt4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-15T21:29:20Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":231,"issue_id":"ft-3kxe.1","author":"CalmOwl","text":"Coordination note: I’m starting wa-3kxe.6 (benchmark/test suite infrastructure) in parallel so your leak/root-cause patches can drop into an existing validation harness. I’ll avoid touching active leak-fix logic and focus on reusable bench/test scaffolding + baseline/proof plumbing.","created_at":"2026-02-14T17:54:12Z"},{"id":232,"issue_id":"ft-3kxe.1","author":"Jeff Emanuel","text":"Coordination update: I created wa-3kxe.7 (assigned to CalmOwl) for an unblocked benchmark harness slice under wa-3kxe. Scope is limited to Criterion/test infrastructure (, , and budget script wiring) so your leak-root-cause patch stream remains isolated.","created_at":"2026-02-14T17:59:35Z"},{"id":233,"issue_id":"ft-3kxe.1","author":"Jeff Emanuel","text":"Correction to prior comment (shell escaped path text). wa-3kxe.7 scope is only benchmark/test infra: crates/frankenterm-core/benches + crates/frankenterm-core/benches/bench_common.rs + scripts/check_bench_budgets.sh. No edits planned in leak-fix source paths.","created_at":"2026-02-14T17:59:39Z"},{"id":234,"issue_id":"ft-3kxe.1","author":"Jeff Emanuel","text":"Status update: wa-3kxe.7 completed and closed. The benchmark slice is in place and does not touch leak-fix implementation paths. New target covers ring buffer path, diff snapshot capture path, and telemetry instrumentation overhead; you can plug memory-leak patch assertions into this harness as needed.","created_at":"2026-02-14T18:12:02Z"},{"id":551,"issue_id":"ft-3kxe.1","author":"Jeff Emanuel","text":"CreamTower slice update (2026-02-20): implemented bounded short-DCS parser memory guard in frankenterm/escape-parser/src/parser/mod.rs by adding MAX_SHORT_DCS_BYTES (8 MiB), a discarding_short_dcs flag, capped accumulation in dcs_put, and state reset in dcs_hook/dcs_unhook. Added regression test overlong_short_dcs_is_discarded to verify payload capping and parser recovery for subsequent short-DCS sequences. Validation via rch exec: cargo fmt --check PASS; cargo check --workspace --all-targets PASS; cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings fails on pre-existing unrelated lint in crates/frankenterm-core/src/workflows.rs:8548 (redundant_closure); targeted cargo clippy -p frankenterm-escape-parser --all-targets --features std -- -D warnings PASS; targeted cargo test -p frankenterm-escape-parser --features std overlong_short_dcs_is_discarded -- --nocapture PASS. Also updated docs/profiling/wa-3kxe-memory-leak-investigation.md with this session evidence.","created_at":"2026-02-20T17:34:08Z"}]}

 {"id":"ft-3kxe.2","title":"cgroups v2 memory budget and OOM prevention","description":"# cgroups v2 Memory Budget and OOM Prevention

## Skills: /extreme-software-optimization

## Goal
Use cgroups v2 on Linux (and equivalent macOS mechanisms) to enforce per-pane memory budgets, preventing the mux server from being OOM-killed.

## Linux: cgroups v2
Create a cgroup hierarchy for FrankenTerm:
- /sys/fs/cgroup/frankenterm/ — top-level cgroup for mux server
- /sys/fs/cgroup/frankenterm/pane-<id>/ — per-pane child cgroups
- Set memory.max per pane cgroup (configurable, default 1GB)
- Set memory.high as soft limit (triggers throttling, not kill)
- Monitor memory.current for per-pane RSS tracking

## macOS Alternative
macOS lacks cgroups. Use advisory memory management:
- Jetsam priority adjustment via memorystatus_control (if available)
- Per-pane jemalloc arena with budget tracking (from wa-3axa)
- If pane arena exceeds budget: warn, pause captures, suggest cleanup
- OOM prevention: protect mux server via lower OOM score (Jetsam priority)
- Memory pressure monitoring via os_proc_available_memory() and vm_statistics64

## Implementation
```rust
#[cfg(target_os = "linux")]
mod cgroup {
    pub fn create_pane_cgroup(pane_id: PaneId, budget_bytes: u64) -> Result<()> { ... }
    pub fn destroy_pane_cgroup(pane_id: PaneId) -> Result<()> { ... }
    pub fn read_memory_current(pane_id: PaneId) -> Result<u64> { ... }
}

#[cfg(target_os = "macos")]
mod memory_budget {
    pub fn create_pane_budget(pane_id: PaneId, budget_bytes: u64) -> Result<()> { ... }
    pub fn check_pane_usage(pane_id: PaneId) -> Result<u64> { ... }
}
```

## Tests
- **Linux**: Create cgroup, set memory.max, verify enforcement
- **Linux**: Pane exceeding budget triggers throttling (memory.high)
- **macOS**: Arena budget tracking matches expected usage
- **macOS**: Memory pressure detection triggers warnings
- **Criterion benchmarks**: cgroup stat read <50μs per pane
- Test cleanup: all cgroups destroyed on pane close

## Acceptance criteria
- Per-pane memory budgets enforced (cgroups v2 on Linux, advisory on macOS)
- Mux server protected from OOM kill
- Per-pane RSS tracking without /proc parsing (via cgroup stats or arena stats)
- Graceful handling when cgroups v2 not available (fall back to advisory)
- Works on both Linux and macOS","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","assignee":"WildBeaver","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:24:11.756980Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-11T07:39:20.719589Z","closed_at":"2026-02-11T07:39:20.719589Z","close_reason":"Implemented memory_budget.rs with cgroups v2 per-pane hierarchy (Linux) and advisory budget tracking (macOS).  {"id":"ft-3kxe.3","title":"Differential snapshots for continuous background saving","description":"# Differential Snapshots for Continuous Background Saving

## Skills: /extreme-software-optimization

## Goal
Implement differential (incremental) snapshots that capture only CHANGED state since the last snapshot, reducing I/O and CPU overhead for continuous background saving.

## How it works
1. Maintain a "dirty set" of pane IDs whose state changed since last snapshot
2. On snapshot:
   - Only capture panes in the dirty set
   - Write diff records (old_value → new_value) for changed fields
   - Store diffs as a chain: base_snapshot → diff1 → diff2 → ...
3. Periodically compact: merge diff chain into new base snapshot

## Dirty tracking
- Mark pane dirty on: output received, resize, title change, process exit
- Mark clean after: pane is successfully captured in a diff snapshot
- Track at field granularity: scrollback dirty vs metadata dirty

## Diff record format
```rust
pub enum SnapshotDiff {
    PaneScrollbackChanged { pane_id: PaneId, new_lines: Range<usize> },
    PaneMetadataChanged { pane_id: PaneId, fields: Vec<(String, DynValue)> },
    PaneCreated { pane_id: PaneId, snapshot: PaneSnapshot },
    PaneClosed { pane_id: PaneId },
    LayoutChanged { tab_id: TabId, new_layout: Layout },
}
```

## Restore from diff chain
1. Load base snapshot
2. Apply diffs in order
3. Result: complete state at the time of last diff

## Relationship to WAL (wa-283h4.2)
Differential snapshots are the SIMPLER version of continuous persistence:
- Diff snapshots: periodic (every 5 min), captures changed state
- WAL: continuous, captures every mutation
- If WAL is implemented, diff snapshots become redundant
- Implement diff snapshots first (simpler), WAL later (optimal)

## Tests
- Create base snapshot, modify 3 panes, take diff → verify only 3 diffs recorded
- Restore from base + diff chain → verify identical to current state
- **proptest**: Random sequence of modifications + snapshots → restore always matches current state
- **Criterion benchmarks**: diff snapshot with 200 panes, 5 dirty → target <50ms
- Compaction: chain of 10 diffs compacts into single base → restore still works
- Edge case: pane created after base, closed before next snapshot

## Acceptance criteria
- Dirty tracking at field granularity
- Diff-only snapshots for changed panes
- Restore from diff chain produces correct state
- Compaction reduces chain length without data loss
- <50ms for diff snapshot with 5 dirty panes out of 200","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","assignee":"WildBeaver","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:24:13.192898Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-12T06:12:05.346727Z","closed_at":"2026-02-12T06:12:05.346727Z","close_reason":"Differential snapshot module complete: DirtyTracker, SnapshotDiff, DiffChain, DiffSnapshotEngine with auto-compaction. 36 tests (33 unit + 3 proptest) all passing. Added Criterion benchmarks with 5 groups (capture/restore/compact/tracker/scrollback). All acceptance criteria met.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-3kxe.3","depends_on_id":"ft-29k1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-15T21:29:20Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-3kxe.3","depends_on_id":"ft-3kxe","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-15T21:29:20Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"ft-3kxe.4","title":"Lock-free SPSC ring buffer for capture pipeline","description":"# Lock-Free SPSC Ring Buffer for Capture Pipeline

## Skills: /alien-artifact-coding (concurrency correctness), /extreme-software-optimization

## Goal
Replace the current capture pipeline buffering (Mutex-protected Vec) with a lock-free single-producer single-consumer (SPSC) ring buffer for zero-contention data flow from capture thread to processing thread.

## Why SPSC (not MPMC)
The capture pipeline is strictly producer→consumer:
- Producer: capture thread writes pane output
- Consumer: processing thread reads and indexes
- No contention needed — SPSC is optimal for this pattern
- MPMC (wa-1sm78) is for the event bus where multiple producers exist

## Design
```rust
pub struct SpscRingBuffer<T> {
    buffer: Box<[MaybeUninit<T>]>,
    head: CachePadded<AtomicUsize>,  // Write position (producer)
    tail: CachePadded<AtomicUsize>,  // Read position (consumer)
    capacity: usize,
}
```

Key properties:
- Cache-line padded head/tail to prevent false sharing
- Power-of-2 capacity for fast modulo (bitwise AND)
- No allocations after construction
- Wait-free: both push and pop are O(1)

## Formal Verification (/alien-artifact-coding)
SPSC ring buffer MUST be proven:
- **Linearizable**: Each push/pop appears atomic
- **No data races**: Producer never writes where consumer is reading
- **No lost elements**: Every pushed element is eventually poppable
- **Bounded**: Buffer never exceeds capacity

## Tests
- **Criterion benchmarks**: Push/pop throughput, target >100M ops/sec
- **LabRuntime DPOR**: Schedule exploration for producer/consumer interleavings
- **Loom**: Verify memory ordering correctness under TSO and weaker models
- **proptest**: Random push/pop sequences → verify every pushed element eventually popped
- Stress test: sustained 1GB/sec throughput for 60 seconds
- False sharing test: verify via perf stat that cache misses are near-zero

## Acceptance criteria
- Zero-contention SPSC ring buffer
- Cache-line padded to prevent false sharing
- >100M ops/sec throughput
- Formally verified correctness
- No allocations in steady state
- LabRuntime + Loom verification passes","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","assignee":"StormyIsland","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:24:13.997748Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-13T04:37:07.253877Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T04:36:08.163140Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-3kxe.4","depends_on_id":"ft-3kxe","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-15T21:29:20Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":235,"issue_id":"ft-3kxe.4","author":"StormyIsland","text":"Progress update (StormyIsland): implemented first working slice for lock-free SPSC capture buffering.

Code changes:
- Added new module `crates/frankenterm-core/src/spsc_ring_buffer.rs` using `crossbeam::queue::ArrayQueue` with bounded async `send`/`recv`, close semantics, and depth/capacity helpers.
- Wired runtime to use two-stage handoff: `mpsc ingress -> capture relay (single producer) -> SPSC ring -> persistence consumer`.
- Added relay lifecycle to `RuntimeHandle` (`relay` join handle) and included relay in `join()`/shutdown waits.
- Added `crossbeam.workspace = true` in core Cargo and exported module via `lib.rs`.

Validation run:
- `cargo fmt --check` ✅
- `cargo test --lib spsc_ring_buffer::tests::` ✅ (4 tests passed)
- `cargo check --all-targets` ❌ fails on pre-existing workspace issue in `crates/frankenterm-core/tests/proptest_export.rs` (`E0716` temporary dropped while borrowed) unrelated to this bead.
- `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` ❌ fails on extensive pre-existing warning debt across many modules unrelated to this bead.

Next: I can either continue hardening this slice (loom/proptest + runtime queue metrics for SPSC stage) or switch to closing follow-on acceptance gaps.
